hints.title: Nudge
hints.content: The lookup only tells you 'found' or 'not found'. Can you inject conditions that change which response you get? That boolean difference is your data channel.

hints.title: Direction
hints.content: Try an employee ID you know exists (like 1) and append AND conditions. For example: 1 AND 1=1 should return 'found', while 1 AND 1=2 should return 'not found'. If that works, you have boolean injection.

hints.title: Approach
hints.content: Use SUBSTRING() and ASCII() to test individual characters of the flag. For example: 1 AND ASCII(SUBSTRING((SELECT value FROM secrets LIMIT 1),1,1))>64 — adjust the threshold with binary search to find each character.

hints.title: Near-solution
hints.content: Write a script that iterates over each character position and uses binary search on the ASCII value. For position N, test: 1 AND ASCII(SUBSTRING((SELECT value FROM secrets LIMIT 1),N,1))>M. Narrow down M between 32 and 126 for each position. Stop when SUBSTRING returns empty (length check).

hints.title: Solution
hints.content: Python script approach: 1) First find flag length: 1 AND (SELECT LENGTH(value) FROM secrets LIMIT 1)=N — iterate N until 'found'. 2) For each position 1..N, binary search ASCII value: send GET /lookup?id=1 AND ASCII(SUBSTRING((SELECT value FROM secrets LIMIT 1),{pos},1))>{mid}. If 'found', search upper half; if 'not found', search lower half. 3) Convert ASCII values to characters and concatenate the flag.
